allergens: gluten (possible from fish sauce or batter)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of crispy fish rice with Tom Yam sauce is approximately 400 g, comprising a portion of deep-fried fish fillet (~120 g), steamed jasmine rice (~200 g), and Tom Yam sauce (~80 g).

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Fried fish (SR Legacy #15264), Cooked white rice (SR Legacy #20045), and Thai Tom Yam soup components; weighted composite calculation for a typical restaurant serving.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Thai restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite: approximately 30% fried white fish, 50% steamed jasmine rice, and 20% Tom Yam sauce, using USDA SR Legacy data for each component. Fried fish values drawn from USDA SR Legacy item 15264 (fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) adjusted for frying oil absorption (~8% fat increase). Rice values from USDA SR Legacy item 20045 (rice, white, long-grain, cooked). Tom Yam sauce estimated from USDA SR Legacy components (lemongrass, chilli, fish sauce, lime juice). Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crispy fish rice with Tom Yam sauce. GI estimated at 64 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white jasmine rice, which has a published GI of approximately 64 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). The fish and sauce components contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 32.5) / 100 ≈ 20.8, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 19.8 × 4 = 79.2 g, adjusted for sauce dilution to ~32.5 g net glycemic carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and Tom Yam sauce
hypertension.guidance: Each 400 g serving provides approximately 1240 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced fish sauce or consume a smaller portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white jasmine rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 20.8 per serving is in the moderate range; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion to half and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load and frying oil fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of elevated sodium (~1240 mg per serving) and fat from deep-frying (~16 g per serving) warrants portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease; the fish component provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids which partially offset cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Fish Rice with Tom Yam Sauce is a Thai-inspired rice dish consisting of a deep-fried white fish fillet served over steamed jasmine rice and dressed with a tangy, spicy Tom Yam sauce. The dish is popular in Thai restaurants in Singapore, including at Saap Saap Thai. The fish is battered or coated and fried until golden and crunchy, providing a contrast in texture to the soft, fluffy jasmine rice beneath. The Tom Yam sauce is made from lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, fresh chilli, fish sauce, and lime juice, delivering a characteristic sour-spicy-aromatic flavour profile. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal (~592 kcal per 400 g serving) with a reasonable protein contribution from the fish (~33 g per serving) and a significant carbohydrate load from the rice (~79 g per serving). Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce in the Tom Yam sauce (~1240 mg per serving), which is a concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic load is moderate (estimated GL ~20.8), driven primarily by the white jasmine rice component. The dish contains fish allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. It is generally considered halal. Clinically, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate rice portion size, and those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the sodium and fat content from frying.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite Thai restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite calculation using USDA SR Legacy data for fried fish, steamed jasmine rice, and Tom Yam sauce components. GI is estimated from the analogous white jasmine rice GI value from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation and restaurant-specific variation.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the USDA SR Legacy source components used for estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source database components used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source database components used. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in fried fish and rice components per USDA SR Legacy data. The food_id GD001572 is preserved from the input seed record. The restaurant tag 'Y4' in the original food name appears to be a menu category identifier at Saap Saap Thai - Funan and is not part of the canonical food name. Cuisine tags 'Pad Thai' and 'Thai Noodle' from the input are not applicable to this rice dish and have been excluded from the output. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant plate; actual weight may vary by restaurant. Halal status is assumed based on typical Thai restaurant practice in Singapore but has not been independently verified for this specific restaurant.
